message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 31
Recent Green phase: Strengthened claim validation from presence-based to count-based. Removed isCovered/assertDamagesCoveredByPolicy. Added countByItemType(damages) (counts damages keyed by .itemType, mirrors countByType which counts items by .type) and assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts(policyItems, damages): compares damage counts per type against insured counts per type (countByType(policyItems)), throwing Error(`Too many damages for item type: ${type}`) when damaged[type] > insured[type] (missing → 0). This subsumes the earlier "item not in policy" check (0 insured → 1>0 throws). handleClaim now calls assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts.

Refactor while keeping all 31 tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, then APP mass. 

NOTE possible duplication: countByType(items: Item[]) keys by item.type, and countByItemType(damages: Damage[]) keys by damage.itemType — same shape, different field name. Consider whether a single generic count-by-key helper (e.g. countBy(values, keyFn)) cleanly unifies them, or whether the two are clearer kept separate. Use judgment per Simple Design (Rule 3 no duplication vs Rule 2 reveals intent / Rule 4 fewest elements).

One remaining error-case test after this: negative damage amount → throw (do NOT implement yet). Keep all tests green.

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 31 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the refactoring.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase introduced two functions the prompt flags as potential duplication:

- `countByType(items: Item[])` — counts items keyed by `item.type`
- `countByItemType(damages: Damage[])` — counts damages keyed by `damage.itemType`

**Naming assessment**: The two names are confusingly similar (`countByType` vs `countByItemType`) yet operate on different shapes. This is a naming smell — the names don't reveal *what differs* between them. They differ only in the field they read.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (the duplicated region)

**`countByType` (current):**
```
const counts: Record<string, number> = {};   // binding counts(1) = 1
for (const item of items) {                   // loop(5) + binding item(1) = 6
  counts[item.type] = (counts[item.type] ?? 0) + 1;  // assign(6) + invocations counts[](2)x2=4 + item.type(2)x2=4 + const 1(1) = 15
}
return counts;                                // binding(1) = 1
```
Mass ≈ 23. `countByItemType` is identical in shape → Mass ≈ 23. **Combined ≈ 46.**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

- **Rule 1 (Tests pass)**: ✅ green.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals intent)**: The near-identical names hurt clarity. There is genuine conceptual duplication: "count values grouped by a key."
- **Rule 3 (No duplication)**: Two byte-for-byte identical loop bodies differing only in the key field. Clear DRY violation.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest elements)**: Two functions where one generic helper suffices.

**Decision**: Extract a single generic `countBy(values, key)` helper. This unifies the duplication (Rule 3), removes the confusing twin names (Rule 2), and reduces element count (Rule 4). The key extraction is passed as a function, making the grouping criterion explicit at each call site.

Let me apply the refactoring.

content.text: Now replace `countByItemType` usage. Since `assertDamagesWithinInsuredCounts` is the only caller of `countByItemType`, I'll inline the `countBy` call there and remove the redundant function.

content.text:
All 31 tests still green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**`countBy` (generic helper):**
```
binding counts(1)=1
loop(5) + binding value(1)=6
binding key(1) + invocation keyOf()(2)=3
assign(6) + counts[](2)x2=4 + const 1(1)=11
return binding(1)=1
```
Mass ≈ 22 (one helper).

**`countByType` (thin wrapper):** invocation `countBy`(2) + binding(1) + lambda invocation `.type`... ≈ 5.

**Damage call site:** inline `countBy(damages, (damage) => damage.itemType)` ≈ 5 (replaces the former `countByItemType` body of ≈23 plus its invocation).

**Mass change**: Before ≈ 46 (two ~23 loops). After ≈ 22 (one loop) + ~5 wrapper + ~5 inline lambda ≈ 32. **Net reduction ≈ 46 → 32 (Δ −14).**

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied** — Extract generic `countBy` helper:

- ✅ **Rule 3 (No duplication)**: Eliminated two byte-for-byte identical counting loops. The grouping logic now lives in exactly one place; only the key selector varies per call site.
- ✅ **Rule 2 (Reveals intent)**: Removed the confusingly-similar twin names `countByType` / `countByItemType`. The remaining `countByType` is a one-line wrapper that names the domain operation; the damage path passes `(damage) => damage.itemType` inline, making the grouping key explicit at the call site.
- ✅ **Rule 4 (Fewest elements)**: Three counting constructs (two full functions) reduced to one generic helper plus a thin domain wrapper.
- ✅ **APP mass**: ≈46 → ≈32 (Δ −14) by collapsing two iteration loops into one.

I considered keeping the two separate (Rule 2 can favor a named domain function over a generic abstraction), but here the two were *identical in shape and behavior* and their names actively misled — so unifying improves clarity rather than harming it. I kept the `countByType` wrapper rather than inlining it everywhere because its callers (premium/component logic) read more clearly with the domain name than with a raw lambda.

I left the flagged negative-damage-amount error case unimplemented, as instructed.

## Step 7: Report

```
Refactoring Complete:
Refactoring: Extracted generic countBy(values, keyOf) helper, unifying the
             duplicated countByType / countByItemType counting loops.
Mass Change: ~46 → ~32 (Δ −14)
Tests:       All 31 passing ✅ (7 todo untouched)
```

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_04-34-58_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
